feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

sections.title: PEGI Response to Call for Evidence for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment
path: PEGI Response to Call for Evidence for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment

paragraphs.text: PEGI Pan European Game Information Ref. Ares(2025)9121729 - 24/10/2025

section_path: PEGI Response to Call for Evidence for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment
PEGI Response to Call for Evidence for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment
PEGI Response to Call for Evidence for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment
paragraphs.text: PEGI (Pan European Game Information System) was established as a self and co-regulatory system and reflects an industry that takes the responsibility to protect players very seriously and prioritises children and adults enjoying video games in a safe, fair and fun way. The PEGI system is how publishers apply for, and receive a licence to display, an appropriate age rating for each of their games. These users of the PEGI age rating system also commit to a number of good practices that ensure a high level of privacy, safety and security for players using video game platforms. The PEGI system, established in 2003 at the request of the EU institutions, is now used in 40 European countries. It is actively enforced and is constantly evolving to meet new challenges in the way games are made and how players interact with them.

section_path: PEGI Response to Call for Evidence for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment
paragraphs.text: The PEGI system, and in particular the enforceable Code of Conduct, directly address the issues raised in the Consultation relating to video games. These commitments, which are regularly updated and refined, supplement and work in combination with EU consumer law. As such, there is no need to impose new consumer laws on the video game industry. To do so would be duplicative, cause confusion and remove the ease with which an industry code of practice based on the self and co-regulation model can be easily and quickly adapted to meet new risks associated with the online ecosystem. Rather, the Commission should continue to support, and strengthen the adoption of, the PEGI system, including the PEGI Code of Conduct by: (1) explicitly reaffirming its status as a European system of self and co-regulation for the protection of players applicable to the video games industry which addresses the risks related to the video games industry; and (2) establishing categorically that the PEGI system requirements, including the Code of Conduct, apply on all platforms that make video games available to EU players including those owned or run by companies headquartered outside the EU. PEGI would like to continue its engagement with the Commission and other European institutions to explore the exact process by which these two objectives may be achieved.

section_path: PEGI Response to Call for Evidence for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment
paragraphs.text: The PEGI system was established with active support from the European Commission and Member States following a 2002 Council resolution that recognises self-regulation as one

section_path: PEGI Response to Call for Evidence for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment
paragraphs.text: of the adequate means to support age rating systems for video games. From then until now, video game companies wishing to apply for a PEGI age rating licence must sign a contract committing themselves to age rating and labelling all their video game products with PEGI age ratings and descriptors and to providing safe and transparent online gameplay environments as set out in the PEGI Code of Conduct.

section_path: PEGI Response to Call for Evidence for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment
paragraphs.text: The 2008 Commission Communication on the protection of consumers, in particular minors, in respect of the use of video games "welcomed and supported further efforts to achieve a self-regulatory or co-regulatory cross-media, pan-European age-rating system and called upon the Member States to integrate into their national systems the information and classification system put in place in the framework of the PEGI and PEGI On-line initiatives."³

section_path: PEGI Response to Call for Evidence for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment
paragraphs.text: As recently as 16 October 2025, the Internal Market and Consumer Protection Committee of the European Parliament (IMCO) "Report on the Protection of Minors Online"⁴ highlights "the video games industry's commitment to self-regulation as demonstrated by the implementation of age recommendations systems, which play a role in safeguarding younger audiences and promoting responsible gaming practices" (vote in Committee). The Opinion of the Culture and Education Committee on this file similarly "stresses the need to promote a safe gaming environment and encouraging awareness of Pan-European Game Information (PEGI) ratings and parental controls".

section_path: PEGI Response to Call for Evidence for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment
paragraphs.text: PEGI will continue to evolve and adapt to the changes in the way players interact with video games. PEGI is currently undertaking the necessary investigations and planning to expand its classification criteria in a similar way to that adopted by the USK, the body responsible for the German age rating system for video games. PEGI is considering how online features such as monetisation or player-to-player communication might affect the level of the age rating for a game, when viewed in conjunction with precautionary measures provided by the publisher and the video game platform on which the game is acquired and played.

section_path: PEGI Response to Call for Evidence for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment
paragraphs.text: The PEGI system is recognised by national legal or regulatory frameworks giving PEGI what amounts to be a co-regulatory basis in certain countries. A number of countries have laws providing for mandatory age rating of video games which directly or indirectly recognise PEGI while other countries' age rating boards officially accept PEGI ratings for

section_path: PEGI Response to Call for Evidence for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment
paragraphs.text: video games. This demonstrates that self-regulation and co-regulation is an effective framework for providing guidance and consumer protection in practice. The PEGI system supplements and works alongside EU consumer law and demonstrates it has the flexibility and agility to make consumer protection future-proof. This is illustrated in a 2023 update to the PEGI Code of Conduct which, amongst other things, established four new obligations (the existence of community standards, player grief-reporting mechanisms, privacy policies and probability disclosures for random rewards), compliance with which must be expressly confirmed by publishers before they can receive a licence to use the PEGI age rating. This proportionate principles-based framework allows authorities and industry to respond to new phenomena quickly and effectively. Further, PEGI is staffed and supported by minor protection experts and operates with relatively unbureaucratic procedures, which removes the responsibility for minor protection in gameplay from the government or regulators. Finally, repealing or duplicating mechanisms that have already been successfully implemented and proven effective, such as those of PEGI, would risk undermining both consumer trust and legal certainty.

section_path: PEGI Response to Call for Evidence for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment
paragraphs.text: Therefore, any future EU regulations should build on existing solutions and integrate regulated self- and co-regulation into its framework, thereby pursuing a regulatory approach that is effective in practice, proportionate, and adaptable to technological and design developments. Specifically, PEGI seeks re-affirmation of the self and co-regulatory status of PEGI as a system which can address the risks and concerns related to video games, and calls on the Commission to confirm that the PEGI system must be adopted by all companies who make video games available to EU players. This point is also acknowledged in IMCO Report on the "Protection of Minors Online" (vote in Committee) referred to above which "recalls that all video games supplied in Europe should abide by the Pan European Game Information System's Code of Conduct rules." PEGI would welcome the opportunity to enter into a dialogue with the Commission concerning the status of PEGI and its applicability in the region where it is currently recognised.

section_path: PEGI Response to Call for Evidence for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment
